front: Building: its Carrara marble exterior warped and had to be replaced, a mistake the architect did not repeat at his performing arts center in Essen.
Building: Carrara marble was used for its facade to suggest a connection to the Mediterranean.
Building: in the 1990s, the public voted against using granite to re-clad this Helsinki building after its original marble cladding warped.
Building: designed to be viewed by traffic proceeding down Freedom Avenue, in accordance with the architect's desire to build on Eliel Saarinen's work.
Building: this Helsinki hall includes a congress wing and faces Toolo Bay.
Building: this MIT dormitory was given a serpentine, curving shape so each resident would have a view of the Charles River.
Building: Aalto designed this dormitory while teaching at MIT, describing it as 'a cross between a ski lodge and a ship.'
Building: this MIT dormitory featured a moon garden roof and used curved brickwork in its construction.
Building: rows of grass stairs dot its exterior, between red brick buildings — exemplifying the architect's brick-obsessed 'red period.'
Building: this town hall features a Renaissance-inspired courtyard and is located in Jyvaskyla, considered Aalto's home city.
Building: the open living room of this house features irregularly-spaced thin black steel columns wrapped in bark, echoing the surrounding birch forest.
Building: designed for the Gullichsen family, its kidney-shaped pool inspired Thomas Church's design for the Donnell Garden.
Building: the kidney-shaped pools designed by this architect proved an ideal shape for vert skateboarding, inadvertently influencing its development in Southern California.
Building: Aalto designed a cantilevered plywood chair here to aid tuberculosis patients; the chair's top and bottom curve into loops.
Building: this early functionalist work by Aalto, along with the Turun Sanomat building, exhibits characteristics of the International Style.
Architect: co-founded the furniture company Artek with wife Aino; the company still markets his cantilevered Paimio chair.
Architect: designed an undulating glass vase for the Savoy Restaurant in Helsinki, now known as the Aalto vase.
Architect: IKEA's Poang chair was inspired by this architect's furniture designs.
Architect: designed buildings with wife Aino in a 'total work of art' style, first designing the complete building then adding special features.
Building: the architect used airplane propellers as ceiling fans for this wooden structure, considered one of his better-known critical regionalist projects.

Building: Robert Venturi praised this architect's Cultural Center in Wolfsburg — built around a roof terrace — in Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture, for combining 'standardization with irrationality.'
Building: this architect's German works include a 200-foot highrise in Bremen and a performing arts center (opera house) in Essen.
Building: designed the Vyborg Library, as well as a library at Mount Angel Abbey in Oregon with a brick exterior fan and a curving interior steel frame illuminated by natural light.
Building: begun after his wife's death, this summer house features an external fire pit and wooden beam loft reflecting architectural whimsy.
Building: Helsinki works include the Rautatalo office buildings and, on the same block, a copper and marble bookstore.
Building: the Muuratsalo Experimental House, built in red brick, is part of this architect's 'red period.'
Architect: the Finnish Association of Architects gives an award named for this person; Jorn Utzon received it in 1982.
